name: GIO
usp: Core blockchain infrastructure company behind the Polkadot ecosystem and Substrate framework, whose clients build sovereign blockchains where runtime bugs can halt entire networks.

outreach_message: Parity's clients are building sovereign chains on Substrate, which means a runtime-level decision — a client bug, an upgrade that behaves differently than expected — doesn't just affect one application, it halts an entire network that teams have built their businesses on. Sovereignty at the chain layer is only meaningful if the runtime layer beneath it is equally owned and auditable. We built Kolme from the same instinct — that mission-critical execution requires full infrastructure ownership, not shared dependencies. Worth 20 minutes at EthCC in Cannes to compare notes on where that line sits?

usp: Develops Arbitrum, Ethereum's largest L2 rollup by TVL, processing billions in transactions with fraud proofs and now decentralizing sequencing, where a bug can freeze or drain locked assets.

outreach_message: Akaki — sequencer incentive design is one of the most consequential unsolved problems in L2 architecture, because the economic model you establish for sequencing is a long-lived commitment: it shapes who participates, how value gets captured, and what failure modes become structurally embedded. The risk that's hardest to price in that design process is the one introduced by running sequencer incentive logic on shared infrastructure where the governance of the substrate isn't aligned with the economic model you're trying to enforce. FP Block's Kolme framework gives teams a dedicated sequencing environment with full control over the incentive and ordering substrate — no upstream governance decisions overriding the economic design. Would you have 20 minutes at EthCC in Cannes this June to compare notes on sequencer architecture?

outreach_message: Julie — De Gaulle Fleurance's digital assets practice advises regulated entities on structures where a legal error isn't just a malpractice question; it's a permanent on-chain event that outlives the contract, the transaction, and often the client relationship. The exposure that's hardest to address in structuring work is that your advice sits on top of execution infrastructure your clients don't govern — and when that infrastructure produces an unexpected outcome, the legal permanence of the result is your client's problem, not the network's. FP Block built Kolme so regulated entities can operate on dedicated application chains they fully control, closing the gap between the compliance perimeter your advice establishes and the infrastructure reality beneath it. Would 20 minutes at EthCC in Cannes this June be a useful conversation?

name: ED FELTEN
usp: Develops Arbitrum, Ethereum's largest L2 rollup by TVL, processing billions in transactions with fraud proofs and now decentralizing sequencing, where a bug can freeze or drain locked assets.
icp_reason: Billions in L2 TVL; sequencer or bridge bugs are irreversible
outreach_message: Ed — your work on formal methods and correctness proofs has long centered on a fundamental asymmetry: the cost of proving a system correct upfront is far lower than the cost of recovering from an incorrect system in production, especially when the system's outputs are permanent. Arbitrum's fraud proof design reflects that instinct, but the formal guarantees it provides are bounded by the assumptions of the execution substrate beneath them — and those substrate assumptions are ones the Arbitrum team doesn't fully control once they're committed to shared infrastructure. FP Block's Kolme framework is built on the premise that mission-critical systems need to own their entire execution environment, not just the logic running on top of it. Would you have 20 minutes at EthCC in Cannes this June to talk through where formal guarantees end and infrastructure ownership begins?

name: BIANCA BUZEA
usp: Chronicle Protocol is a verifiable oracle network originally built for MakerDAO, delivering cryptographically attested price feeds to DeFi protocols on-chain.
icp_reason: Oracle feeds underpin collateral pricing; wrong data = protocol insolvency
outreach_message: Chronicle's oracle feeds are only as trustworthy as the full path from source data to on-chain attestation — and if the execution environment those attestations land in isn't owned and controlled by the teams depending on them, the cryptographic proof of correctness doesn't fully close the trust loop. A wrong price that's cryptographically attested is still a wrong price. We built Kolme so protocols depending on verified feeds can own the substrate those feeds settle into, not just trust it. Worth 20 minutes at EthCC in Cannes?
